_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 666 verses across 46 terms

set.analysis_note: Cain's incensed state arises from perceived rejection; it seizes him inwardly and shows outwardly as a fallen countenance, linking the emotion to both the will (he cared deeply about acceptance) and the body.
Cain's anger expresses itself through his face falling, showing that the inner fury has an immediate outward physical signal — the downcast face is the visible collapse of the angered inner person.

God questions Cain directly about his incensed state, probing the inner source of the anger and its bodily sign, implying the emotion is subject to moral examination and is not beyond the person's control.
Strife here is a social-relational breakdown erupting between competing herdsmen over scarce resources, driven by competing interests rather than a single person's inner fury.
Abram appeals to kinship loyalty as the reason strife must not arise between them, framing provocation as something the will can and must refuse for the sake of relational peace.
Abraham pleads that the Lord not become incensed while he presses his intercession further, treating divine anger as a real inner-being state that could end the dialogue and cut off mercy.
Abraham again entreats the Lord not to be incensed as he makes one final petition, showing divine anger as a threshold the intercessor fears crossing and that shapes the limits of bold prayer.
The Philistines' jealousy of Isaac is sparked by his material prosperity; the emotion is directed outward at another's advantage, revealing covetous hostility born from comparison.
Esau's fury is described as something that must 'turn away' over time, portraying rage as a volatile inner state that subsides rather than being reasoned away, requiring distance and waiting.

Rachel's jealousy of Leah's fertility is a consuming inner pain directed at her sister's advantage, so intense it drives a desperate demand — 'give me children or I die' — showing jealousy merging with existential despair.

Jacob's anger is kindled against Rachel because she blames him for barrenness that belongs to God's decision; the incensed response is directed at being held responsible for what is beyond his agency.

Rachel asks Laban not to be incensed at her inability to rise, using the term to appeal for the suppression of anger in the face of a physical explanation, implying anger could be a rational response but should yield to circumstance.

Jacob's anger flares against Laban after being unjustly accused and searched; it drives him to confront Laban verbally with a moral challenge, showing incensed anger channeled into self-defense and indignation at injustice.

The sons of Jacob are incensed because Shechem's act violated a moral order ('such a thing must not be done'), meaning their anger is conscience-driven, arising from outrage at an objective wrong done to their family.
Joseph's brothers are jealous of him due to their father's favoritism; the jealousy is a sustained inner disposition of resentment directed at a sibling's privileged status.
Potiphar's anger is kindled by his wife's accusation against Joseph; the incensed state is triggered by a report that arouses wounded honor, showing anger as an inner reaction to perceived betrayal.

Pharaoh's anger at his officers is the inner-being cause that leads directly to their imprisonment, showing royal anger as a powerful authoritative emotion that translates immediately into punitive action.
The officers' troubled appearance when Joseph sees them reflects their inner distress from disturbing dreams; the term here indicates vexed agitation visible on their faces rather than outright rage.

The cupbearer recalls Pharaoh's anger at his servants as the originating event that led to imprisonment, confirming that royal anger functions as a decisive inner-being force producing severe relational and legal consequences.

Judah entreats Joseph not to let his anger burn against him, acknowledging that incensed anger toward a subordinate is a dangerous inner state requiring careful approach and respectful petition to avert.

Joseph tells his brothers not to be incensed with themselves for selling him, redirecting the anger that would feed guilt inward; he offers a theological reframe (God's providence) as the ground for releasing self-directed anger.
The archers' bitter attacking of Joseph is the outward violent expression of provocation aimed at him — the term names the hostile, embittering pressure exerted against a person by adversaries.

Egypt's making of Israel's lives bitter is a sustained, systemic provocation inflicted through brutal labor; the term names an imposed bitterness that grinds down the inner life and dignity of the people.

The Lord's anger is kindled against Moses for his repeated reluctance; the incensed state arises from the divine will being resisted by Moses' hesitancy, yet God redirects rather than punishes, showing anger constrained by purpose.
God's burning anger is sent out against adversaries as an active, consuming force; here it is the inner divine fury expressed as a weapon that destroys opponents like fire consumes stubble.
Moses is angry at the people for disobeying the command about manna; his anger is a moral response to willful disobedience, showing incensed leadership emotion rooted in conscience and authority.
The quarreling of Israel at Meribah is named as the defining act of the place; it is strife directed not merely against Moses but against God, arising from distrust and testing the Lord's presence.
Egypt's arrogant dealing with Israel is described with a term for boiling insolence; the inner-being content is proud presumption that 'boils over' into oppressive action against God's people.

God's jealousy in the first commandment is constitutive of his identity — a claim of exclusive devotion that brooks no rival; it is directed against idolatry and has generational moral consequences for those who reject it.
The term here describes willful, premeditated attack — a 'boiling' presumption in the will that plans cunning murder; the law targets this inner deliberateness as what makes the act capital.
God's wrath will burn as a direct consequence of oppressing the vulnerable; the incensed state is morally grounded in God's protective concern for the weak and issues in lethal judgment.

Israel is warned not to rebel against God's angel-messenger; the provocation named is willful disobedience that will not be pardoned, showing that provoking God's representative is treated as provoking God himself.

God's wrath burns hot against Israel for the golden calf and is poised for consuming judgment; the incensed state is here both intense and purposeful, seeking to act against the object of its anger unless intercession intervenes.

Moses questions why God's wrath burns against his own redeemed people, appealing to the contradiction between consuming anger and the prior act of salvation — intercession challenges the anger with God's own redemptive identity.

Moses urges God to turn from his burning anger, treating it as a stance God can relent from; the appeal is grounded in concern for God's reputation among the nations, showing anger as an inner state open to persuasion.

Moses' anger burns hot when he sees the calf and dancing; the incensed state is immediately acted upon — he smashes the tablets — showing anger as an inner force that drives decisive, destructive outward action.

Aaron pleads with Moses not to let his anger burn hot, acknowledging the incensed state as one that can be moderated by appeal; he invokes Moses' knowledge of the people's evil tendency as a mitigating factor.
God's very name is declared to be 'Jealous,' making jealousy a defining attribute of his inner being — an exclusive, identity-level claim that totally forbids worship of any other god.

Divine wrath is portrayed as a force that could fall on the whole congregation if the priests' mourning rituals are wrong; anger here is a corporate threat requiring ritual propriety to keep it at bay.
Moses is angry at Eleazar and Ithamar for burning the sin offering improperly; his anger is morally and ritually grounded, arising from failure to follow sacred law at a critical moment.

God's fury is the inner-being force behind the sevenfold discipline he promises to inflict on a disobedient Israel; the rage is purposeful and proportional, expressed as covenant punishment for persistent rebellion.

Wrath is an impersonal dangerous force that could break out upon Israel if unauthorized persons approach the tabernacle; the Levites' guard duty is the structural safeguard against this deadly anger being triggered.

A husband's jealousy toward his wife is described as a spirit that comes over him, locating it in the inner person as an involuntary, possessing force — regardless of whether the wife is actually guilty.

The spirit of jealousy is identified as the inner-being impulse driving the husband's suspicion; it is named twice, underscoring it as a real inner state whether or not infidelity has actually occurred.

The grain offering brought in cases of suspected infidelity is called a 'jealousy offering'; the offering ritualizes and memorializes the husband's inner jealousy before God, giving the emotion a formal judicial and sacred function.

The grain offering of jealousy is held by the priest during the ordeal ritual, connecting the husband's inner jealousy to a sacred legal procedure administered before the Lord, where divine judgment will adjudicate the emotion's validity.

The priest waves the jealousy offering before the Lord as a formal sacred act, showing that the husband's jealousy has been transferred into a liturgical object presented to God for his determination.

Jealousy is named as the defining inner-being condition that triggers a specific law; the legal category exists because jealousy in marriage is treated as a real, serious, and judicially actionable inner state.

The spirit of jealousy that comes over a man is identified as the initiating inner-being force that sets the entire legal-ritual process in motion; it is treated as a state requiring divine adjudication.
Jealousy is again named as the spirit that impels the husband to bring his wife before the Lord; it is the inner-being cause whose truth or error only the sacred ordeal can reveal.
God's incensed anger is kindled by the people's complaining and expresses itself outwardly as consuming fire in the camp, showing it as a reactive, punishing force directed against ingratitude.

The Lord's anger blazes hotly in response to widespread weeping among the people, while Moses' inner displeasure mirrors it at a human level, showing the emotion as intensified by repeated provocation.

Moses challenges Joshua's jealousy as misplaced protectiveness on Moses' behalf, contrasting it with Moses' own wish that God's Spirit would rest on all, exposing jealousy here as a narrowing, self-referential impulse.

God's anger is kindled against the people mid-feast, before they finish consuming the meat, showing it as swift and unrelenting judgment, striking the body in plague even as desire is still being satisfied.

God's anger is kindled against Miriam and Aaron for their challenge to Moses, and its immediate expression is withdrawal — he departs — showing incensed anger acting through sudden absence and rejection.
Moses' intense anger at Korah's rebellion prompts him to appeal to his own integrity before God, making the anger here a morally grounded inner response to being unjustly challenged in leadership.

Moses and Aaron intercede by questioning whether God's anger would justly consume the whole congregation for one man's sin, framing divine anger as a force that must be examined for proportionality before it strikes.
God's wrath is described as already having 'gone out' — an active, moving force unleashing plague — and atonement through incense is the only thing that can arrest its outward momentum.
Priestly guard duty over the sanctuary and altar is framed as a preventive measure so God's wrath never again falls on Israel, showing wrath as a danger that faithful priestly vigilance holds at bay.

God's anger is kindled because Balaam went on his journey, and it manifests as the angel of the Lord blocking his path, showing divine incensed anger expressing itself through active, physical obstruction.

Balaam's anger is kindled when the donkey lies down under him, and he immediately strikes her — showing the emotion as impulsive, frustration-driven inner fire that discharges at once into physical violence.
The verse does not directly evidence inner-being content for za.am; Balaam is recounting Balak's request to curse Israel, but no indignation is expressed or located in any person here.

Balaam's rhetorical question implies that denouncing Israel is impossible without God's own indignation as its source, showing za.am as a divine inner disposition that must authorize any effective curse.

Balak's anger is kindled against Balaam for blessing instead of cursing, and it erupts in a physical gesture — striking hands together — showing anger here as a felt betrayal discharging into visible agitation.

Israel's spiritual unfaithfulness through Baal worship kindles God's anger against the nation, showing incensed anger as a direct response to covenant betrayal involving the will and allegiance of the people.

God's burning anger is so intense it must be 'turned away' through public execution of the guilty chiefs, showing fierce anger as a sustained, consuming inner disposition that demands visible atonement before it relents.

God's rage (wrath) against Israel's idolatry was so consuming it would have destroyed the nation, but Phinehas's act of zeal matched it and turned it back, showing divine rage as a total, destructive inner force that zealous action alone could satisfy.

Phinehas's jealousy is described as sharing the same inner quality as God's own jealousy, making his act of zeal not personal rivalry but a mirroring of divine possessive love outraged by Israel's unfaithfulness.

God's jealousy is the motivating force behind his near-destruction of Israel, and Phinehas channeling that same jealousy averts it, showing jealousy as a fierce inner claim of exclusive devotion that, if matched, becomes redemptive.

Phinehas is honored with a perpetual priestly covenant because his jealousy was 'for his God' — directing an exclusive, protective inner passion toward God's honor — and this act made atonement for the people.
Meribah is named as the place where Israel's quarreling provoked God, and Moses and Aaron's failure there — failing to uphold God's holiness — is the direct reason they are barred from the land.
Moses' anger is directed at military commanders who spared women after battle against Midian, showing anger here as a morally motivated inner response to perceived disobedience of God's instructions.

The Lord's anger was kindled on the specific day the spies returned with their fearful report, and his oath followed immediately, showing divine anger as a decisive inner response to faithlessness that locked in its consequences.

God's incensed anger against Israel's unfaithfulness resulted in forty years of wilderness wandering until the guilty generation died, showing sustained anger as a disposition that shapes Israel's entire historical experience.

The new generation is described as intensifying God's fierce, burning anger against Israel by repeating their fathers' sin, showing burning anger as a cumulative inner fire that grows hotter with repeated provocation.
Moses describes Israel's strife as a crushing burden he cannot bear alone, showing inner conflict between the people as a weight that exhausts leadership and demands shared governance.

God's anger is triggered by hearing the people's fearful, faithless words and immediately results in a sworn oath of judgment, showing divine anger as a listening, responsive inner state that issues in binding consequences.
The Lord's anger fell on Moses himself on account of Israel's rebellion, showing that divine anger at a people's sin can extend to affect even the intercessor who stands among them.
Israel acted presumptuously — with an inner boiling-over of self-will — by defying God's command and going up to battle anyway, showing 'to boil' as an inner presumption that overrides obedience.

God's anger at Moses was roused on Israel's account, resulting in Moses being barred from the promised land, showing divine anger as a directed, consequential inner state with lasting, covenantal effect on individuals.
God's jealousy is paired with the image of a consuming fire, presenting it as an intensely possessive inner attribute that destroys what competes for exclusive allegiance owed to him.
Making idols is framed as an act that provokes God to anger, showing that human inner corruption — expressed in idolatry — acts as a direct stimulus that arouses God's inner anger against his people.

God declares himself a jealous God whose jealousy punishes children for their fathers' unfaithfulness across generations, showing divine jealousy as a deeply possessive inner attribute with far-reaching, transgenerational consequences.

God's jealousy is the ground of the warning — because he is jealous, his anger can be kindled and bring destruction — showing that divine jealousy and divine anger are causally linked inner attributes.
God's jealousy is described as actively present in Israel's midst, making it an immanent, watchful inner attribute that guards exclusive devotion and whose violation provokes destructive anger.

Intermarriage leading sons into other gods' service is the specific trigger for God's kindled anger in this verse, showing divine incensed anger as a jealous, protective response to the defection of Israel's will.

Moses commands Israel to remember provoking God to wrath throughout the wilderness journey, framing divine anger as something deliberately stirred by human rebellion and requiring active memory to guard against repeating.

At Horeb Israel provoked God and his anger reached the point where he was ready to destroy them entirely, showing divine anger as an inner intensity with destructive potential only barely held in check.
God's anger at Horeb is so intense it reaches the threshold of consuming the whole people, showing it as a fully aroused inner state capable of total judgment when provoked at its core.

Moses' forty-day fast and prostration are motivated by Israel's evil acts that provoked God to anger, showing that human sin stimulates God's anger and that intercessory suffering is the means of addressing it.

Moses feared God's hot displeasure and rage directed at Israel, a fear intense enough to drive his intercession — showing divine rage here as a terrifying, felt threat that moves even the intercessor to action.

God's anger and hot displeasure bear against Israel with destructive intent, and only Moses' prayer turns it aside, showing divine anger as a sustained, personal inner bearing-against that intercession must actively address.
God's anger at Aaron individually was so severe he was near destruction, showing divine anger as capable of focusing on a specific person and requiring specific intercession to avert.

Multiple named sites — Taberah, Massah, Kibroth-hattaavah — are recalled as occasions where Israel provoked God to wrath, showing divine anger as a pattern repeatedly aroused by Israel's persistent rebellion.

God's anger being kindled against idolatry would result in withheld rain and failed harvests, showing divine incensed anger as an inner response to covenant breach that expresses itself in withdrawal of creation's blessing.

God's fierce, burning anger must be fully 'turned from' before mercy and compassion can flow, showing burning anger as an inner state that blocks blessing and must be wholly satisfied before relational restoration occurs.

Public punishment of the presumptuous person is intended to make all the people hear, fear, and no longer act with inner boiling presumption, showing the remedy for self-willed insubordination as fear instilled by visible consequence.

The prophet who presumes to speak words God has not commanded acts out of an inner boiling self-will that overrides divine authority, and the penalty is death — showing presumptuous inner assertion as a capital offense.

God's jealousy smokes against the individual who secretly serves other gods, paired with his anger and written curses, showing jealousy as an intensely focused inner claim that singles out and punishes the defector without mercy.

The desolation of the land is attributed to God's anger and rage, with the Sodom and Gomorrah overthrow as the analogy, showing divine rage as the inner force behind total, irreversible destruction of what refuses him.
God's incensed inner state is the direct cause of covenant curses falling on the land; the burning is not passive but drives active, sweeping judgment against Israel's unfaithfulness.
Rage here is one of three intensifying terms (anger, fury, great wrath) describing God's inner heat that propelled the violent uprooting and exile of Israel from their land.
Wrath is the climactic, greatest-magnitude element in a triad of divine emotional intensity that drove Israel's total displacement from the land into exile among foreign nations.
God's incensed anger, once kindled, expresses itself inwardly through withdrawal—hiding his face—and outwardly through abandonment, letting evils overwhelm the people as consequence.
Israel's idolatrous deeds are described as the direct stimulus that provokes God's anger; the provocation originates in willful human action ('work of your hands') directed against God.
Abominable idol worship is the specific cause that provokes God's anger; the provocation is paired with stirring jealousy, showing both compete as inner responses to Israel's betrayal.
Israel's worship of strange gods stirs God's jealousy, an inner reaction rooted in his exclusive claim on their loyalty; the jealousy is provoked by the affront of rival devotion.
The vexation produced by his own sons and daughters is what moved God inwardly to spurn Israel; the inner irritation arose from the provocation of those in closest covenant relationship.
Israel provoked God to anger through idols, and God mirrors the provocation back—he will provoke Israel's anger using a foolish nation, showing anger as a relational, retaliatory dynamic.
God's jealousy is stirred by Israel worshipping a non-god; he will reciprocate by making Israel jealous through a non-people, framing jealousy as both a divine wound and a weapon of judgment.
Here God restrains judgment out of concern that enemy provocation—enemies misreading Israel's defeat as their own triumph—would distort the truth of divine agency; inner caution moderates wrath.
One man's covenant breach causes God's anger to burn against the whole people of Israel, showing the incensed state as a corporate-level inner response to communal defilement.
God's burning anger is portrayed as something he turns away from once justice is enacted; the memorial heap marks both the intensity of that anger and the moment of its resolution.
Wrath is anticipated as a consequence that would fall on the leaders themselves if they broke a sworn oath; it functions here as a feared punitive inner force restrained by covenant obligation.
God's anger is warned to be imminent and communal—one tribe's potential rebellion would trigger his wrath against the entire congregation of Israel the very next day.
Achan's individual breach brought wrath that fell on all Israel, illustrating that this wrath is not contained to the offender but spreads corporately across the covenant community.
Covenant transgression and idolatry are the conditions that kindle God's incensed anger, which then expresses itself as swift destruction, removing Israel from the land he gave them.
God's jealousy is identified as a defining constitutional attribute of his holiness; it means he will not overlook unfaithfulness, directly linking jealousy to the refusal to forgive sins.
Israel's abandonment of God for foreign gods is the direct act that provokes the Lord to anger; the provocation flows from communal idolatrous worship replacing covenant loyalty.
God's incensed anger moved him inwardly to an active decision—giving Israel over to plunderers and enemies—making the kindled anger the inner spring of external covenant discipline.
God's incensed anger is linked to a stated reason—Israel's transgression of the covenant commandments—and is accompanied by verbal judgment, showing anger as a reasoned, principled inner response.
The kindling of God's anger against Israel results directly in national subjugation; the incensed inner state translates into concrete political and social bondage lasting eight years.
Gideon pleads that God's anger not burn against him before making a second request, acknowledging the risk that repeated testing could kindle divine displeasure directed personally at him.
Zebul's anger is kindled by hearing a rival's inflammatory words; the incensed state is a human, politically motivated reaction to a perceived threat to his ruler's authority.
The provocation here is social and political—Gaal stirs up the city against Abimelech; the inner dynamic is the deliberate agitation of communal resentment to destabilize loyalty.
God's kindled anger against Israel again results in national subjugation under two foreign powers; the incensed inner state is the divine motive behind military and political punishment.
The strife here is an inter-tribal military conflict with the Ammonites; Jephthah describes it as a great dispute in which he sought Ephraimite help that never came, compounding the grievance.
Samson's hot anger after being betrayed on the riddle is Spirit-empowered and leads directly to violent action; the incensed state drives him to strike down thirty men and return home furiously.

Naomi uses 'bitter' to describe the intense inner anguish she feels at the Lord's hand acting against her; the provocation is not human but divine suffering that makes her situation unbearable for others' sake.

Naomi internalizes God's bitter dealings as her very identity, asking to be called Mara (bitterness); the inner state of being deeply afflicted by God is worn as a name that expresses her soul's condition.
Peninnah deliberately and repeatedly provokes Hannah's deep grief over her barrenness; the provocation is targeted, intentional, and designed to aggravate an existing inner wound.
The vexation Hannah suffers is not spontaneous but the result of a rival's sustained, grievous irritation; the inner distress is deliberately inflicted and linked to her inability to have children.
The annual provoking of Hannah at the Lord's house produces an inner sorrow so intense she cannot eat; the provocation's recurring nature deepens the vexation to an unbearable, embodied grief.
Hannah describes her prayer as pouring out from great anxiety and vexation deep within her; the inner agitation is the source and fuel of her desperate, fervent speech before God.
Saul's anger is greatly kindled when the Spirit of God rushes upon him; the incensed state is Spirit-empowered, transforming news of injustice into fierce, action-driving inner fire.
Samuel's anger arises from moral and theological indignation at Saul's disobedience; unlike impulsive rage, it drives him to intercede all night, showing an anger bound to covenantal zeal.
Eliab's anger against David is personally directed and accusatory; it rises from wounded pride and suspicion, expressed immediately in sharp words that attack David's motives and character.

Saul's anger is sparked by a perceived slight to his status; the inner state is inseparable from jealousy and wounded honor, immediately producing a thought that spirals toward fear of losing the kingdom.
Saul's anger is treated as a diagnostic signal Jonathan can read; if incensed, it reveals that harm toward David is already decided in Saul's will, linking the inner anger to a formed lethal intent.

Saul's anger toward Jonathan is kindled by perceived betrayal in loyalty to David; it erupts in shaming speech attacking Jonathan's honor and his mother, showing anger fused with wounded pride and rage.

David appeals to God as judge over his strife with Saul, placing the conflict in God's hands rather than taking personal revenge; the inner posture is one of aggrieved restraint seeking divine vindication.
David sees God's avenging of the insult from Nabal as resolution of a personal strife; God's judgment removes the grievance and simultaneously keeps David from the sin of self-vindication.
God's burning anger against Amalek was fierce enough to require total execution; Saul's failure to carry it out is now the reason God's own fierce inner wrath turns against Saul himself.
The Philistine commanders' anger at Achish for bringing David stems from strategic distrust; the inner displeasure is rationally grounded in fear that David would become a battlefield adversary.

Abner's great anger at Ish-bosheth's accusation rises from a sense of wounded loyalty and honor; his incensed state is fueled by the injustice of being charged with treachery despite his devoted service.
God's anger kindles against Uzzah for touching the ark in error; the incensed inner response is immediate and results in death, showing anger as a holy, self-enforcing reaction to ritual violation.
David's anger at God's breaking out against Uzzah is a human reaction of confusion and distress; it arises not from self-interest but from the jarring unexpectedness of divine holy judgment.
The king's rage is anticipated as a volatile inner response to news of battlefield recklessness; Joab prepares the messenger to redirect that anger by preemptively referencing Uriah's death.

David's anger is greatly kindled against the man in Nathan's parable, ironically directed at his own sin; the incensed inner state is morally sharp but self-unaware, making it a vehicle of Nathan's confrontation.

David's great anger upon hearing of Amnon's rape of Tamar is an internal reaction that, notably, does not translate into action or discipline, leaving the incensed state inwardly contained and unresolved.
The men of Judah challenge why the Israelites are incensed, implying the anger is unwarranted — an inner flaring directed at a perceived slight over the king's loyalty that has no factual basis.
Saul's zeal for Israel drove him to violently target the Gibeonites; this jealous inner drive was nationalistic in object, overriding a sworn covenant and producing destructive action.
God's being incensed is portrayed as a cosmic force — the earth and heavens shake as the direct physical consequence of his inner anger, showing its vast, world-shaking intensity.
Strife here is relational conflict within David's own people — an inner-social rupture from which God delivered him, establishing his leadership over nations instead.
The Lord's anger is kindled against Israel and issues outward in a sovereign act — inciting David to number the people — showing wrath operating through directed, consequential will.
God's anger at sin is presented as a judicial inner response that, when activated, results in exile — it is conditional, triggered by moral failure and expressed in punitive action.

God's anger with Solomon is rooted in Solomon's heart having turned away — the anger is a moral response to spiritual apostasy, specifically directed at one who had received personal divine revelation.
Idolatry and the making of images are described as provoking God to anger — the provocative act reaches into God's inner being, stirring indignation by rejection and contemptuous dismissal of him.
Israel's making of Asherim is the act that provokes the Lord to anger, which then results in uprooting and scattering — the provocation is the inner cause, divine judgment the outer consequence.
Judah's sins provoke God to jealousy — the inner being-response named is jealousy, stirred by Judah's spiritual unfaithfulness, surpassing even prior generations in intensity.
Jeroboam's sin-laden leadership is characterized as provoking the Lord's anger — the provocation is cumulative, generated by a leader who caused a whole nation to sin and incite divine wrath.
The vexation (anger) of the Lord is presented as the active inner response to Jeroboam's sin-inducing leadership — it is the emotional-judicial state that makes divine judgment intelligible.
Despite divine elevation, Baasha walked in Jeroboam's sinful path and so provoked God to anger through the people's sins — the provocation flows from moral failure in a leader entrusted with Israel.

Baasha's evil works provoked God to anger — the inner affront is stirred by Baasha's deeds and his imitation of Jeroboam's corruption, making his condemnation the direct outcome of sustained provocation.
Baasha and Elah's idol-driven sins provoked the Lord to anger — idols are identified as the specific instrument of provocation, stirring God's inner indignation against Israel's leadership.
Omri's continuation of Jeroboam's sin-pattern provoked God to anger through idols — the provocation is a repeated, willful pattern that accumulates divine indignation against Israel.
Ahab's making of an Asherah surpassed all prior kings in provoking God to anger — the verse marks a superlative level of provocation, intensifying the inner affront to God beyond any predecessor.
Elijah describes an intense inner zeal for the Lord — a jealous, consuming passion for God's honor — that is not private but drives his lone stand against Israel's apostasy and covenant-breaking.
Elijah repeats his declaration of being very jealous for the Lord, revealing that this burning inner devotion to God's cause persists even in exhaustion and fear, grounding his prophetic identity.
The king of Israel returns home 'vexed and sullen' after a rebuke — the vexation is an inner sullen displeasure that manifests in withdrawal, reflecting wounded pride or frustrated will.

Ahab's vexed inner state over Naboth's refusal leads to withdrawal, lying down, and refusing food — the vexation is a brooding, sullen anger that shuts down normal life and signals deep inner disturbance.
God declares judgment on Ahab's house citing the anger Ahab provoked him to — the provocation is Ahab's personal affront to God that elicits a wrath-driven judicial response and makes Israel sin.
The anger of God provoked by Ahab is the named inner-judicial state that grounds the sentence against his house — vexation is the affronted inner response motivating divine punishment.
Ahaziah's Baal worship provoked the Lord to anger in every way his father had — this total, comprehensive provocation of God's inner anger represents inherited and intensified spiritual rebellion.
Great wrath comes against Israel following the Moabite king's sacrifice of his son — this wrath appears as an overwhelming, externally-experienced force that compels Israel's military withdrawal.

The Shunammite's 'bitter distress' is the inner anguish of a grieving mother — the term here names the depth of her inner suffering rather than anger directed outward, though the root shares a provocation range.
Naaman's anger flares at Elisha's failure to come out in person — the anger is wounded pride and offended expectation, driving him to walk away before any healing.
Naaman's rage over Elisha's simple remedy drives him to physically turn and leave — the rage is inner contempt and indignation at perceived disrespect, expressed in decisive bodily withdrawal.
Jehu invites Jonadab to witness his zeal for the Lord — the jealous inner passion is here claimed as righteous motivation for Jehu's acts of judgment, presented as devotion to God's honor.

The Lord's anger is kindled against Israel and results in their being handed over to oppressive foreign rulers — the inner anger is sustained ('continually'), producing prolonged disciplinary consequence.

Elisha's anger at the king's insufficient striking of the arrows is a morally-charged inner displeasure — the prophet's indignation flows from knowing the king's half-hearted action will limit Israel's deliverance.

Israel's wicked high-place offerings are named as provoking the Lord to anger — the idolatrous acts are the inner affront that stirs divine indignation, framing judgment as a response to accumulated provocation.

Child sacrifice, divination, and selling oneself to evil collectively provoke God to anger — the provocation is extreme, involving the most grievous acts, making the inner affront to God total and inexcusable.
The Lord's very great anger with Israel results in their removal from his sight — the anger is at its peak intensity ('very angry'), producing the ultimate relational and territorial rupture: exile.
The Lord's zeal is the inner driving force that will produce the remnant's survival — it is not provoked anger but passionate divine commitment that ensures Jerusalem's deliverance.
Manasseh's occult and sacrificial evils are collectively named as provoking God to anger — each transgression compounded the inner affront, with the full catalog of sin as the ground of divine wrath.
Israel's persistent evil since the Exodus has provoked God to anger — the provocation is historically deep and ongoing, rooting his anger in a long record of cumulative moral offense.
Josiah describes the Lord's wrath as 'great' and already kindled against Judah due to disobedience — the inner intensity of divine rage is a burning response to prolonged neglect of God's word.

God's wrath is kindled by idolatrous worship and will not be quenched — the inner anger is presented as irreversible at this point, its intensity uncontainable precisely because the provocation was deliberate and sustained.

The intent behind the idolatry was explicitly to provoke God to anger — this verse reveals the provocation as willful inner defiance, not ignorance, making the resulting wrath a fully warranted response.

The kings of Israel provoked the Lord to anger through high-place shrines — Josiah's destruction of them is the corrective act against a long legacy of cultic provocation that stirred divine indignation.

God's incensed anger is described as burning and great, not relented even by Josiah's reform — the inner intensity of wrath, ignited by Manasseh's provocations, is so deep it transcends a single generation's repentance.

The 'burning of his great wrath' describes the fiery, intense quality of God's inner anger — the term captures wrath at its most heated, a consuming inner fire directed against Judah for Manasseh's offenses.

Manasseh's provocations are cited as the cause of God's unrelenting wrath — the repeated provocations stirred an anger so deep that even Josiah's faithful reforms could not avert its ultimate expression.

The 'provocations' of Manasseh are the accumulated affronts that produced God's burning wrath — vexation here names the divine inner state of aroused indignation that remains undiminished despite Josiah's faithfulness.
The Lord's anger is kindled against Uzzah instantly for touching the ark — the inner indignation responds to a specific boundary violation and issues in immediate deadly judgment.
David's anger is aroused at God's sudden act against Uzzah — the inner incensed state is a reactive response to an unexpected divine judgment, reflecting David's disturbed and troubled will.

Wrath came upon Israel because of the census — the wrath is a divine inner displeasure that strikes Israel as consequence, and its severity is signaled by the incompleteness of the count and its exclusion from records.

This verse lists disasters including enemies besieging Israel in their gates — in context the enemy siege is one form of pressure or affliction that may arise; the term 'tsur' (provoke/besiege) here refers to external distress rather than inner anger content directly evidenced in this verse.

God's anger at sin is conditional and judicial — when provoked by moral failure, it results in the people being handed to enemies and carried into exile, making anger the hinge between sin and consequence.
God's rage is a destructive force that would have been 'poured out' on Jerusalem; humility by the people moves him to withhold it, showing the inner wrath responds to contrition.
Asa's rage toward the seer is an inner state that drives outward cruelty—imprisoning the prophet and inflicting harm on others, showing rage as an impulse that overrides restraint.
Asa's anger toward the seer is the inner provocation that triggers his punitive actions; his being 'angry' is what the term names as the emotional source of his harsh response.
God's wrath is a moral consequence that 'goes out' against Jehoshaphat for allying with those who hate God, functioning as a divine inner-disposition directed by ethical judgment.
Wrath here is a potential consequence that the judges must help the people avoid by proper instruction; it is God's reactive disposition that incurring guilt will trigger.
God's wrath comes upon Judah and Jerusalem as a direct result of their abandoning him for idols; it is his inner disposition of condemnation made active against persistent apostasy.
The Ephraimite troops become intensely incensed when dismissed without pay or share in battle; their burning anger is an inner reaction to perceived injustice that drives them home furiously.
The Lord's anger is kindled against Amaziah for worshipping conquered gods, prompting a prophetic rebuke; the incensed divine response is directed at the king's foolish idolatry.
Uzziah's enraged inner state at being confronted by the priests immediately precedes God's judgment of leprosy, showing his fury as a self-willed defiance that precipitates divine punishment.
Israel's rage in slaughtering Judah was so extreme it is described as reaching to heaven, marking the inner fury as a morally excessive, heaven-observed force unleashed in battle.
The rage with which Israel killed their Judahite brothers is said to have reached heaven, characterizing the inner fury as so extreme it registers as a sin before God.
The Lord's fierce burning anger rests upon Israel because of their actions against Judah; the prophet urges release of captives as the necessary response to this intense divine wrath.
Fierce burning anger is already upon Israel due to accumulated sin; bringing more captives would compound the guilt, showing the burning anger as an escalating divine response to moral failure.
Ahaz's idolatry in every city of Judah deliberately provokes God to anger; the inner-being dynamic is that each act of false worship is an irritant directed against God's exclusive claim.
God's wrath came upon Judah and Jerusalem making them a horror and disgrace; it is his active inner condemnation expressed through historical catastrophe as punishment for unfaithfulness.
Hezekiah's heart-decision to make a covenant with God is explicitly aimed at turning away God's fierce burning anger; the anger is positioned as something that responds to renewed covenant loyalty.
Yielding to God and serving him is the required human action to cause his fierce anger to turn away; the burning anger is conditional on stiff-necked resistance and responsive to submission.
Hezekiah's proud heart triggered God's wrath against him and Jerusalem; the wrath is a divine inner reaction to the king's ingratitude and pride after receiving God's benefit.
Hezekiah's humbling of his proud heart caused God's wrath to not come upon them; the wrath is a reactive divine disposition that is directly moderated by human humility.
Manasseh's multiplied occult evils are described as provoking God to anger; each practice is an irritant that stirs God's inner displeasure, with the cumulative effect intensifying that response.
The king describes God's wrath as 'great' and already 'poured out' because the fathers disobeyed the Law; the rage is an accumulated divine response to long-term covenant violation.
God declares his wrath will be poured out and not quenched because idolatry was done with deliberate intent to provoke him; the rage is his unrelenting response to willful apostasy.
The people's idolatrous works are described as having the intent to provoke God to anger; the provocation is willful, indicating the inner dynamic of deliberate rebellion stirring divine wrath.
God's wrath rose against his people incrementally as they persistently mocked prophets; it is depicted as a building inner force that finally reaches the point where no remedy remains.
The fathers enraged the God of heaven, resulting in the Babylonian exile and destruction of the temple; the enraging is the human act that provoked God's retributive inner response.
The decree warns that failure to support God's house may cause his wrath to fall on the king and his realm; the wrath is a potential divine inner disposition triggered by neglect of worship.
Ezra fears God's anger would be so consuming that it would leave no remnant if commandments are broken again; the anger is a total, eliminative inner disposition provoked by renewed unfaithfulness.
The assembly plans an orderly process to address mixed marriages specifically in order to turn away the fierce burning anger of God; the anger is the pressing motivation requiring urgent resolution.
Sanballat becomes angry and greatly incensed upon hearing of the wall-building; the inner fury is an immediate emotional reaction to Israel's progress, expressed outwardly in mockery.
Sanballat's state of being 'greatly enraged' is what the term captures; the wall-building provokes his inner anger to an intensified pitch that results in jeering and opposition.
Nehemiah calls God to remember the enemies' sin because they have provoked him to anger before the builders; the provocation is a deliberate affront to God witnessed in the context of his work.
The coalition of surrounding enemies becomes very angry as Jerusalem's walls are visibly repaired; their incensed inner state is a collective hostility reaction to the restoration of the city.
Nehemiah himself becomes very angry when he hears the outcry of the oppressed poor; his incensed inner state is a righteous indignation triggered by injustice among his own people.
The Egyptians 'acted arrogantly'—the boiling inner pride that drove Pharaoh and his people to defy God—which God saw and responded to with signs and wonders against them.
The ancestors 'acted presumptuously' with a stiffened neck, refusing God's commands; the inner boiling quality describes a self-willed, arrogant defiance that hardened into rebellion.
Even after warnings, they acted presumptuously again, combining inner arrogance with a stubborn shoulder and stiff neck; the boiling willfulness is a persistent inner refusal to submit.

Nehemiah warns that Sabbath-breaking is bringing more burning anger on Israel, just as the fathers' sins did; the fierce anger is a recurring divine response to covenant violation in every generation.
The king's rage burns within him after Vashti's refusal; the inner fire is located explicitly 'within him,' marking it as a deep inner state of wounded royal pride that inflames his whole being.
The king became enraged at Vashti's refusal; his anger is an immediate, intense inner reaction to public defiance of royal authority, burning within him as an agitated inner state.
Wrath is predicted as a social and domestic consequence that will spread through the realm once noble women hear of the queen's defiance; it names an expected storm of displeasure and conflict.
King Ahasuerus's rage had 'abated' after the Vashti episode; its subsiding allows memory and reflection to return, showing rage as a consuming inner state that temporarily displaces other faculties.
Two eunuchs became angry and plotted to kill the king; their anger is an inner state that moves directly to violent intent, showing wrath as a dangerous force that drives toward lethal action.
Haman is 'filled with fury' when Mordecai refuses to bow; the rage fills his inner being completely, implying it takes possession of him and will drive all subsequent planning against Mordecai.

Haman's wrath is stirred by Mordecai's unmoved posture; it fills him internally and is directed specifically at Mordecai, contrasting sharply with his earlier joy and revealing the fragility of his pride.

The king's wrath drives him physically from the banquet into the garden; it is an inner agitation that compels movement and signals that harm is being deliberated, showing rage as an action-propelling force.
The king's wrath 'abated' only after Haman was executed; the inner fury required a concrete act of justice to be satisfied, showing rage as a state sustained until its object is removed.
Jealousy is paired with vexation as a lethal inner force that 'slays the simple'; it is an inward corrosive emotion that destroys the person who harbors it, not merely those it targets.

Job describes God's arrows as carrying poison his spirit drinks; this 'rage' is experienced as an inner spiritual poisoning, a toxic assault on the self that mirrors God's overwhelming fury against him.

Job uses 'strife' in the legal sense of an argued case; here it refers to his reasoned pleading before God and his friends, depicting inner conflict externalized as formal disputation seeking justice.
God's wrath is kindled against Job and he is counted as an adversary; the inner incensed state of God is experienced by Job as a relational rupture in which God treats him as an enemy.
Rage here is God's punishing wrath expressed through the sword—an outward force that brings judgment upon those who wrong the innocent, motivating fear in the wrongdoer.
God's burning anger is directed against the greedy wicked man and enters his very body as divine punishment, making it an internal, physically experienced retribution.
The Almighty's rage is pictured as a bitter cup the wicked are made to drink—a personal, experiential judgment that the sufferer wishes the guilty would face directly.
God's action has made Job's soul bitter—an inner wound to the will and emotional core caused by perceived divine injustice in withholding Job's right.
The term here refers to a legal dispute or cause; Job portrays himself as one who championed the cause of those he did not know, opposing injustice on behalf of strangers.
The dispute here is a grievance brought by servants against their master—a conflict of rights within a household, testing the inner moral response of the one in power.
Job calls for his adversary's formal indictment—the dispute has become a legal confrontation demanding a written charge, reflecting the intensity of Job's desire for vindication.
Elihu's being incensed is rooted in moral indignation—his inner spirit burns because Job justified himself before God, stirring righteous anger at perceived theological error.
Elihu burns with anger at Job's three friends because their inability to answer Job while still condemning him stirred his inner sense of moral failure and intellectual frustration.
Seeing no adequate answer from the three men ignites Elihu's anger—the incensed state arises from observation of failure, triggering an inner compulsion to speak.
Rage here is portrayed as an inward enticement—it can seduce a person into scoffing, showing its capacity to distort judgment and lead the inner will astray.
God's being incensed burns against Eliphaz and his friends because they spoke falsely about God—divine anger here is a moral response to theological dishonesty.
God's burning fury is expressed in terrifying speech directed at rebellious rulers—it is an outward manifestation of divine wrath that dismays and overwhelms its objects.
The Son's anger is quickly kindled against those who refuse to submit—it is a latent inner disposition that flares rapidly into destructive judgment, making deference urgent.
God's wrath here is the feared medium of rebuke and discipline—the psalmist pleads that God's correction not be carried out through this fierce inner disposition against him.
Vexation here manifests physically—grief caused by enemies wastes the eye and weakens the body, showing how inner anguish from oppression produces visible bodily deterioration.
God's indignation is presented as a constant, daily disposition—not an occasional emotion but a persistent inner moral stance that the righteous judge maintains against wickedness.
The term here names those who vex or oppress; the verse contrasts the oppressor's aggression with God as a stronghold, showing the oppressed find shelter from those who trouble them.
Trouble here is the condition produced by oppressors; the psalmist cries out that God seems hidden precisely when vexing enemies press hardest, intensifying the inner anguish of abandonment.
God sees and notes vexation—the inner distress caused by wrongdoers does not escape divine perception; God takes it into his hands as the helper of the helpless.
God's being incensed triggers cosmic upheaval—earth and mountains tremble because of his anger, showing that divine wrath's inner intensity has overwhelming outward, physical consequences.
Strife with the people describes the hostile conflict from which God delivered the psalmist—it is the external relational friction of contention that God resolved by granting dominion.
Grief here wastes the eye and drains soul and body—vexation caused by distress and foes penetrates every level of the inner person, producing comprehensive physical and spiritual exhaustion.
Strife of tongues names hostile verbal attacks from others; God shelters his people from this contentious assault, making his presence a refuge from relational aggression.
The psalmist calls on God to take up his cause—the dispute is a legal conflict requiring divine advocacy, where the inner appeal is for God to act as defender and judge.
Fretting over evildoers names an inner agitation akin to being incensed; the command not to fret warns against allowing the prosperity of the wicked to inflame one's inner state.
Envying wrongdoers is a misplaced jealousy directed at the wicked for their apparent success—the verse commands restraint of this inner covetous response that destabilizes trust in God.
Fretting over the prospering evildoer is an inner agitation that disrupts patient waiting on God—the command is to still this heated inner unrest and trust divine timing.
Rage is named alongside anger as something to be relinquished—it is presented as a disposition that tends only toward evil, making its abandonment essential to moral integrity.
The heated inner state of fretting is commanded to cease because it leads only to evil—inward agitation that mirrors indignation must be forsaken to avoid its destructive moral outcome.
God's wrath is the feared medium of discipline—the psalmist pleads that God's corrective action not be channeled through this fierce disposition, fearing its crushing weight.
God's wrath is the basis of rebuke; the psalmist dreads being disciplined through it, treating it as an overwhelming divine disposition that makes correction unbearable.
God's indignation has a direct physical consequence—it removes soundness from flesh and health from bones, showing that divine moral displeasure penetrates the sufferer's body.
The psalmist asks God to defend his cause against an ungodly, deceitful people—the dispute is an unjust conflict requiring divine vindication as the only recourse.
Strife is observed as a destructive force within the city alongside violence—the psalmist prays God would disrupt and scatter those whose contention corrupts communal life.
God's wrath is called upon to consume enemies entirely—it functions as a total, annihilating force that would demonstrate divine sovereignty over all the earth.
God's anger is experienced as rejection and broken defenses—it is an outward withdrawal of divine protection that the community has suffered and now pleads to have reversed.
Zeal for God's house has consumed the psalmist from within—this jealous ardor absorbs him so completely that others' reproach of God falls upon him personally.
Divine indignation is called to be poured out upon enemies—it is presented as a stored inner force ready to be unleashed, overtaking the guilty with its burning intensity.
God's burning anger is paired with indignation and called to overtake the wicked—it is the active, pursuing dimension of divine wrath that catches up with its objects.
The psalmist was envious of the arrogant because of their prosperity—this jealous inner state arose from comparing his own condition to the wicked's outward success, threatening his faith.
God is called to defend his own cause against those who scoff at him—the dispute belongs to God himself, and the psalmist urges divine action in this ongoing affront.
Human rage is shown to be under God's sovereign control—even the wrath of man ultimately serves divine praise, and what remains God restrains and wears as a garment of authority.
God's rage, though fully warranted, is repeatedly held back—his compassion leads him to atone for iniquity and restrain rather than unleash his full wrathful capacity against Israel.
Indignation is listed as one component in a cluster of divine wrath unleashed on Egypt—it is a distinct inner dimension of God's anger that joins wrath and distress in bringing destruction.

God's burning anger is the primary force let loose upon Egypt, accompanied by wrath, indignation, and distress—it is the fierce interior heat of divine judgment that drives the plague of destroying angels.
Israel's idolatry at high places provoked God to anger—their unfaithfulness stirred his inner moral response of wrath, making idol worship the direct cause of divine displeasure.
Israel's idols moved God to jealousy—their worship of competing gods provoked his exclusive-claim inner disposition, showing that idolatry directly triggers divine jealous response.
God's anger is questioned for its duration—the psalmist asks how long this divine disposition will persist, experiencing it as an enduring state that has not yet turned to restoration.
God's jealousy burns like fire and is paired with ongoing anger—it is an intense inner exclusive-loyalty passion that, when provoked, produces consuming, prolonged divine displeasure.
God's rage is directed outward toward nations that neither know him nor call on his name; the psalmist petitions for it to be poured out on them rather than on Israel.
God's burning anger is portrayed as something that can be turned away and withdrawn; its cessation is the basis for Israel's hope of restored favor.
God's indignation is directed at his people and needs to be put away for restoration to happen; the psalmist pleads for its removal as a condition of salvation.
God's anger is questioned in its duration—will it be forever, across all generations?—revealing it as a sustained inner disposition that has relational consequences for his people.
God's rage is experienced as a crushing physical and emotional weight pressing down on the psalmist, leaving him overwhelmed as if by surging waves.
God's burning anger is felt as a sweeping, destroying force that assaults the psalmist from within; its impact is described as dreadful and personally devastating.
God's rage is depicted as an enduring, intense fire burning against his anointed; the psalmist laments its unrelenting duration and God's apparent hiddenness alongside it.
God's rage produces a state of dismay and complete undoing in those it strikes; it operates in tandem with his anger to bring human life to its end.
God's indignation is the cause of the psalmist's suffering—being lifted up and thrown down—showing it as a force that produces violent reversal of one's condition.
God's wrath, paired with his indignation, is assigned as the reason the psalmist has been violently cast down; it operates as the active agent behind his affliction.
Men in the camp harbored jealousy toward Moses and Aaron, directing envy at those set apart as holy; the jealousy was a communal inner attitude turned against divinely chosen leaders.
God's rage was directed toward destroying Israel, but Moses' intercession turned it away; the verse shows the rage as potentially lethal but capable of being diverted by advocacy.
Israel's deeds provoked God to anger, triggering a plague; the provocation is the human action that stirs up divine wrath, with immediate destructive consequence.
Israel angered God at Meribah through their contention, and this anger spilled over to affect even Moses; it shows anger aroused by human rebellion producing harm beyond its immediate cause.
The Lord's anger was kindled against his own people as a response to their persistent unfaithfulness, resulting in his abhorrence of them; incensed feeling led to relational rupture.
The wicked man is provoked to anger upon seeing the righteous prosper; this vexation is an involuntary inner reaction to the good fortune of others, paired with visible gnashing and wasting away.
A scorching, burning indignation seizes the psalmist from within when he sees the wicked abandon God's law; it is an intense, consuming inner heat provoked by moral outrage.
The psalmist's zeal for God's words consumes him inwardly because his enemies disregard them; this jealous passion operates as a devouring inner fire directed at protecting God's honor.
The psalmist calls on God to plead his legal cause; here 'riv' denotes a formal dispute or lawsuit in which the psalmist is embattled and needs a divine advocate.
Enemies' anger was kindled against Israel to the point of consuming them alive; the verse frames this incensed rage as a near-fatal external threat from which only God's intervention saved them.
This verse describes God hemming the psalmist in on all sides with his hand upon him; it does not evidently express provocation or inner contention in this context.
The command warns against envying a violent man; jealousy here is an inward disposition of coveting another's power or lifestyle that leads one to imitate destructive ways.
Jealousy ignites a man's rage to the point where he shows no mercy in taking revenge; the rage is the explosive, unsparing inner fire produced by jealousy's wound.
Jealousy is named as the force that makes a man furious and drives him to merciless revenge; it is an inner passion that generates uncontrollable anger and retaliatory action.

A fool's vexation is instantly visible, betraying his inner irritation without restraint; the verse contrasts this with the prudent person who conceals offence, showing vexation as a self-exposing inner reaction.
Strife is the invariable product of insolence, while wisdom belongs to those who accept counsel; the verse roots strife in a proud inner refusal to submit to others' input.
Envy is contrasted with a tranquil heart—where tranquility gives life, envy rots the bones; jealousy is a deeply corrosive inner state that physically and vitally destroys the person who harbors it.
Rage can be turned away by a soft answer but is stirred up by harsh words; the verse shows rage as a volatile inner state highly responsive to the relational tone of speech directed at it.
A hot-tempered man's inner rage stirs up strife around him, while the slow-to-anger person quiets conflict; the verse identifies rage as the relational agitator whose presence escalates contention.
Strife is what a hot-tempered man generates and what a patient man quiets; it is the relational conflict that flares when anger is uncontrolled and subsides when anger is restrained.
A king's rage is a lethal force—a messenger of death—but a wise man works to appease it; the verse presents royal rage as an inner disposition with life-and-death power over others.

Strife is set against quiet as the defining evil of a household; even abundance with strife is worse than a dry crust with peace, showing strife as a toxic relational atmosphere that poisons everything.

Strife is like water breaking through a dam—once started it cannot be contained; the verse urges abandoning it at the first sign, showing it as an inner-relational force with escalating, destructive momentum.
Loving strife is equated with loving transgression; strife is here an inner appetite—a willful disposition toward conflict—that is morally aligned with sin and leads to self-destruction.
A foolish son is a grief and a source of bitterness; the vexation is the painful inner distress caused in the parent by a child's folly, felt as both grief and bitter anguish.

A fool's lips lead him into a fight and invite physical punishment; strife here is the conflict that a fool's undisciplined speech inevitably produces, with the inner folly expressed through contentious words.

The one who presents their case first appears right until the other party cross-examines them; 'riv' here refers to a legal dispute or case, where the inner dynamic is one of contested claims needing scrutiny.
When a man's own folly ruins him, his heart rages against the Lord rather than himself; the heart is the seat of this misdirected enraging inner reaction, displacing blame onto God.
A king's rage is compared to a lion's growl—threatening and fearsome—contrasted with the life-giving gentleness of his favor; rage is a powerful inner state with intimidating outward force.

A man of great rage bears consequences he cannot escape, and rescuing him only perpetuates the cycle; the verse shows rage as a deeply ingrained inner disposition that repeatedly brings its bearer into trouble.
Keeping away from strife is honorable, while only fools rush into quarreling; the verse frames strife as an inner-relational pull that the wise resist and the foolish cannot avoid.
A hidden bribe can appease strong rage; the verse treats rage as an inner force that can be secretly pacified, showing it as a formidable but manageable disposition.
Living with a quarrelsome and fretful woman is so vexatious that isolation in a desert is preferable; the vexation is a constant, wearing inner irritation caused by contentious companionship.
The Lord's indignation toward a man is the inner disposition that leaves him vulnerable to the trap of the forbidden woman; divine displeasure is the condition that leads to the man's downfall.

God will plead the cause of the poor against those who exploit them; 'riv' here is a legal contest in which the Lord takes up the disadvantaged party's case, turning their strife into divine advocacy.
One is warned against befriending a man given to rage, because association with a wrathful person risks absorbing their destructive inner patterns; rage is infectious through close companionship.
God as the strong Redeemer will plead the cause of the vulnerable against those who wrong them; 'riv' denotes the legal-adversarial dispute in which God himself becomes the champion.
The heart is warned not to envy sinners; jealousy of the wicked is an inner disposition of the heart that diverts one from fearing God and must be actively resisted.
The reader is told not to envy evil men or desire their company; jealousy here is an inner attraction toward the wicked that the will must resist, lest one be drawn into evil.
The reader is told not to fret or become internally agitated over evildoers; the inner disposition of being incensed at the wicked is something to be deliberately restrained rather than indulged.
The inner impulse to envy evildoers is warned against; the verse treats envy of the wicked as a corrosive inner disposition to be consciously resisted.

The verse does not describe the inner experience of indignation directly; it predicts that peoples will abhor the unjust judge, situating communal moral outrage as the consequence of false vindication.
Strife is framed here as something that can be kept contained by limiting a dispute to the parties involved; exposing secrets to outsiders needlessly fans and widens the conflict.
A backbiting tongue inevitably produces angry looks in the one hearing it, showing that slander triggers indignant displeasure as a natural relational consequence.
Inserting oneself into another's quarrel is depicted as reckless self-endangerment, warning that strife foreign to you inflicts harm on whoever seizes it carelessly.
A quarrelsome person functions like fuel that feeds fire; the verse locates the source of strife in a person's inner disposition that actively sustains and intensifies conflict.
A fool's provocation is heavier than stone or sand, portraying the vexation a fool inflicts on others as a uniquely oppressive inner burden, harder to bear than any physical weight.
Rage is called cruel and anger overwhelming, but jealousy surpasses both; the verse ranks jealousy as the most devastating of these inner passions, one no one can withstand.
Jealousy is presented as the most irresistible of destructive passions, exceeding even wrath and anger in force, with no one able to stand before it.
Rage is an inner disposition that actively generates social disorder; a person given to it stirs up strife and multiplies others' wrongdoing as a direct outflow of that inner state.
Just as physical pressure on milk or a nose produces a tangible result, sustained pressure on anger inevitably produces strife; conflict is the inescapable outcome of intensified anger.
Vexation is here the inner anguish that accompanies great wisdom; increased knowledge brings increased inner distress, making vexation the painful companion of intellectual depth.
Vexation permeates the toiling person's entire inner life; even at night the heart finds no rest, showing that this vexation occupies the heart and robs the person of any inner peace.
Envy of a neighbor is identified as the hidden driving motive behind all human toil and skilled labor; the jealous inner impulse to match or surpass others fuels the entire cycle of work.
God's anger is provoked by the person's rash words and broken vows; the verse warns that careless speech triggers divine wrath that destroys the offender's work.
Vexation is one component of the darkened inner experience of one who hoards wealth; it accompanies sickness and anger as the chronic inner condition of a life lived in futility.
Wrath is listed alongside vexation and sickness as part of the inner misery characterizing the miser's existence; it describes a chronic angry inner state accompanying a life of futile toil.
Vexation (sorrow) is presented as inwardly beneficial; the sadness it brings to the face paradoxically produces gladness in the heart, suggesting that this inner pain is morally formative.
The verse warns against being hasty in spirit to become angry, locating the provocation to anger in the spirit; anger then settles as a resident in the heart of fools.
Vexation/anger is portrayed as taking up residence in the heart of fools; the verse locates it constitutionally in the heart and treats it as a mark of inner foolishness.
In death, jealousy ceases entirely along with love and hate; the verse treats jealousy as a strictly this-life passion of the inner person that perishes with the individual.
Vexation is explicitly located in the heart and commanded to be removed from it; the verse treats it as a conquerable inner occupant that the will can and should expel.
The incensed anger of the speaker's brothers is directed at her personally and results in a coercive action; their inner fury moved them to impose unwanted labor on her.
Jealousy is likened to the grave in its fierce, unyielding intensity and to fire in its consuming force; it is the most relentless and powerful expression of passionate love's inner drive.

The term 'to provoke' here refers to enclosing or besieging with boards; this verse does not evidence inner-being anger content—the term describes a physical protective action, not an emotional state.
The widow's cause (strife/legal plea) does not reach the corrupt rulers; the verse shows that strife here is the legal grievance of the vulnerable, suppressed by leaders who love bribes over justice.

The Lord's anger was kindled inwardly against his people and expressed outwardly in devastating judgment; crucially, even after striking them, his anger has not turned away, showing its sustained, unrelenting intensity.
Hunger and distress trigger a raging, contemptuous fury directed upward at king and God; the verse shows anger arising from desperate need and venting itself in bitter, defiant speech.
The Lord's zeal/jealousy is the inner passionate commitment that drives him to accomplish the promised kingdom; it is the motivating divine energy behind establishing righteous, endless rule.
God's indignation is the inner fury that Assyria is made to serve as an instrument; the staff Assyria wields is described as the very expression of God's furious indignation against his people.

God's indignation is portrayed as time-limited; very soon it will exhaust itself and then turn against Assyria, showing that divine indignation has a determined inner trajectory toward a specific end.

Ephraim's jealousy of Judah is depicted as an inter-tribal rivalry that will be abolished in the coming messianic age; the verse promises the removal of this destructive competitive inner disposition.
Ephraim's jealousy of Judah is a source of inter-tribal hostility that the messianic age will eliminate; jealousy here is an inner divisive passion whose departure enables national unity.
God's anger toward the speaker has turned away and given place to comfort; the verse presents divine anger as an inner disposition that can relent, being replaced by restored relationship.
God's indignation here is the inner energy behind the weapons of judgment he deploys; the instruments of war come expressly as embodiments of his indignation to destroy the whole land.
The day of the Lord is characterized by fierce burning anger coming with cruel wrath; this inner divine passion is the force behind the desolation of the land and the destruction of sinners.
The Lord's fierce burning anger is so intense it shakes the entire cosmos; the verse presents this inner divine wrath as a force that destabilizes creation itself at its peak expression.

The prophet weeps bitter tears over Jerusalem's destruction; the bitterness here is an intense inner grief and anguish rather than provocative anger toward another, expressed as a plea to be left to mourn.
The Lord's zeal/jealousy is directed passionately in favor of his people; it is an inner protective ardor that the people's enemies fail to perceive until the fire of judgment consumes them.
God's indignation is a passing temporal storm that his people can survive by hiding briefly; the verse treats divine indignation as an inner fury that runs its course and then subsides.
God declares he has no wrath toward his vineyard; the verse contrasts the absence of divine rage with a readiness to battle thorns and briers, presenting rage as a disposition consciously set aside.
God's indignation is expressed through burning anger and fury that fill his very lips and tongue; the verse vividly embodies divine inner fury as consuming speech and fire.
God's furious anger accompanies the display of his descending arm in catastrophic judgment; rage here is the inner divine emotional state that charges the destructive theophany with its force.
The Lord's fury is directed against all nations, driving him to devote them to slaughter; it is the inner passionate anger that translates into total, comprehensive judgment over the entire world.
The Lord's wrath is directed outward against all nations; it is the inner driving force behind his enraged action of handing them over to destruction and slaughter.
Strife/cause here is the legal cause of Zion for which God takes vengeance; God's judgment day is framed as his advocacy in a legal dispute on Zion's behalf against her adversaries.
The Lord's zeal/jealousy is the inner passionate commitment on Zion's behalf that guarantees the survival of a remnant; it is the driving divine ardor that accomplishes what no human effort could.
Hezekiah's great bitterness was a painful inner experience that God turned to his welfare; the verse frames bitter inner anguish as divinely purposeful, redeemed by deliverance and forgiveness.

Those incensed against God's servant are moved by an inner hostility that will result in their shame and destruction; the verse shows that being incensed against the righteous leads to the aggressor's ruin.
