Paul's spirit was provoked deep within him—a sharp inner distress and indignation stirred by the sight of Athens filled with idols, locating the provocation in the spirit/inner person.
God's wrath is an active, revealed force directed from heaven against human ungodliness and unrighteousness, specifically against those whose inner will suppresses the truth.
Strife is listed among vices that fill those given over to sin, portraying it as an inner disposition of the corrupt person that produces relational conflict and social disorder.
A hard and impenitent heart actively accumulates wrath as a mounting store of divine judgment, linking the inner condition of stubborn unbelief directly to the coming day of God's righteous wrath.
Self-seeking rivalry, rooted in the will that refuses truth and chooses unrighteousness, is the inner disposition that draws wrath and fury as its just consequence.
Wrath here is the divine outcome awaiting those whose inner orientation is self-seeking and rebellious against truth, presented as the inevitable counterpart to their chosen unrighteousness.
The law produces wrath by exposing transgression; where law is absent there is no transgression to incur it, showing wrath as the judicial result triggered by law's encounter with human failure.
Justification by Christ's blood provides rescue from coming divine wrath, positioning wrath as the peril from which the inner person, now declared righteous, is delivered.
God's wrath is something he desires to display against vessels prepared for destruction, yet he withholds it with great patience, revealing wrath as purposeful divine judgment held in restraint.
God deliberately provokes Israel to anger through a foolish nation, using angry indignation as an instrument directed against Israel's heart to awaken them to their own rejection of God.

Believers are told not to execute personal vengeance but to yield to God's wrath, which belongs to him alone as the proper agent of repayment—so wrath functions as divine prerogative, not human emotion here.
The governing authority acts as God's agent executing wrath on wrongdoers, showing wrath as a concrete, punitive force channeled through human institutions for the deterrence of evil.
Subjection to governing authority is motivated both by fear of its wrath and by inner conscience, contrasting the external threat of wrath with the internal moral faculty of conscience.

Quarreling is listed alongside drunkenness and sexual immorality as behavior of the darkness that believers must renounce, identifying it as an inner disposition incompatible with honorable, daylight conduct.
Quarreling exists as an actual, reported condition among the Corinthian believers, indicating that divisive inner conflict has broken out in their community relationships.
Jealousy and strife among the Corinthians are evidence of a fleshly inner condition, proving they have not matured spiritually but are still driven by merely human, carnal impulses.

A dispute-loving disposition—the inner inclination to be contentious—is named as something to be checked; it finds no support in apostolic or church practice, marking it as an inner tendency to be resisted.
Love is defined in part by its refusal to be provoked or irritated; being unprovokable is an inner quality of love, contrasting it with an irritable disposition that takes offense.
Indignation is one of several strong inner responses that godly grief produced in the Corinthians—a righteous moral anger against the wrong done, proving their sincere repentance.
The Corinthians' zeal positively stirred up (provoked/irritated) most of the Macedonians, showing the term used here in a constructive sense—enthusiasm that kindles eagerness in others.
Rivalry appears in a cluster of relational vices Paul fears finding at Corinth, depicting it as a competitive, faction-driven inner orientation that fractures the community.
Quarreling heads a list of relational sins Paul dreads encountering, presenting it as an inner disputatious spirit that produces communal disorder when he arrives.
Rivalries are named as a work of the flesh, placing self-seeking competitive ambition among the sinful inner drives that the flesh produces and the Spirit opposes.
Strife appears as a work of the flesh alongside enmity and jealousy, identifying it as an inner drive of the sinful nature that generates hostile conflict in relationships.
Humans are described as children of wrath by nature, meaning the entire inner constitution of fallen humanity is oriented toward deserving divine wrath before redemption.

Anger itself is not forbidden—the command 'be angry' acknowledges it as a legitimate inner response—but it must not be allowed to persist and cross into sin, requiring inner governance over its duration.
The command not to let the sun go down on one's anger warns against allowing anger to become entrenched overnight, showing anger as an inner state that must be resolved before it hardens.
Wrath is listed with bitterness, anger, clamor, and slander as destructive inner-relational attitudes that must be completely removed, identified as belonging to the old self that grieves the Spirit.
God's wrath falls on the sons of disobedience as a consequence of moral failure, serving as a warning against being deceived by empty words that minimize the inner seriousness of sin.

Fathers are warned not to provoke their children to anger through harsh treatment; the command protects the child's inner emotional world from being wrongly stirred to resentment by parental misuse of authority.

Rivalry is paired with envy as a corrupt inner motive that drives some to preach Christ, contrasted with good will—showing that the same outward act can arise from a competitive, self-serving inner spirit.
Selfish ambition here is the inner motive of those who proclaim Christ insincerely, intending to cause Paul personal pain—rivalry as a self-serving drive that distorts even outwardly good activity.
Selfish ambition is to be the ground of nothing believers do; the inner drive toward competitive self-promotion is directly opposed to the humility that counts others more significant.
God's wrath is coming as the direct consequence of the vices just listed; it is presented as an active divine response set in motion by human sin, imminent and certain.
Anger is listed among inner attitudes—alongside wrath, malice, slander, and obscene speech—that must be stripped off as part of putting away the old self and its destructive relational patterns.
Fathers are warned not to provoke/irritate their children, because persistent parental provocation damages the child's inner spirit and leads to discouragement—a broken, deflated will.
Jesus is identified as the one who delivers believers from coming wrath, showing wrath as the future divine judgment from which the inner person rescued by the Son is spared.
Wrath has already come upon those who hindered the gospel, presenting it as a realized divine retribution that falls on those who fill up the measure of their sins.
God's purpose for believers is not wrath but salvation, establishing wrath as the destiny of those outside Christ from which God has appointed his people to be exempt.
Men are to pray with holy hands free from anger and quarreling; the inner state of anger is incompatible with genuine, holy prayer, making inner peace a prerequisite for proper worship.
Quarrels about words arise from an unhealthy inner craving for controversy rooted in conceit; they are products of a corrupt inner appetite that generates envy and dissension.
Word-quarrels spring from an unhealthy inner craving for controversy in a person puffed up with conceit; such disputes are internally driven by pride and produce destructive social outcomes.

Quick-temperedness disqualifies a person from church leadership; it is listed as an inner character flaw—an easily triggered anger that is incompatible with the measured, self-controlled temperament God's steward must have.
Quarrels about the law are to be avoided as unprofitable and worthless; they are depicted as contentious disputes arising from foolish inner preoccupations rather than genuine spiritual benefit.
God's wrath is the affective state that grounded his oath excluding the wilderness generation from rest, showing wrath as the settled divine disposition that produces definitive, lasting exclusion.
The oath sworn in God's wrath that barred Israel from rest is quoted again to warn believers; wrath here is the divine inner resolve that permanently closes rest to the disobedient.

An oath functions to end human disputes by providing final confirmation; the verse does not evidence inner-being content for the term but uses 'dispute' to describe the social conflict that oaths are designed to settle.

The verse uses 'beyond dispute' to assert an undeniable axiom about the greater blessing the lesser; it evidences no inner-being content for the term but treats dispute as an intellectual category of contention that this truth exceeds.
Christ endured the active hostility and contradiction of sinners against himself; believers are urged to fix their minds on this so that inner weariness and faintheartedness do not overcome them.
Wrath is presented as an inner tendency to be deliberately restrained—every person should be slow to let it rise, pairing it against the disciplines of careful listening and measured speech.
Human wrath is exposed as internally incapable of producing God's righteousness; its inner drive works against, not toward, the moral outcome God requires.
Selfish rivalry is located in the heart alongside bitter jealousy; its inner presence leads to boasting and falsifying the truth, revealing it as a will-driven disposition that corrupts judgment.
Where selfish rivalry takes hold, its inner effect radiates outward into disorder and every vile practice, showing that this inner disposition structurally produces social and moral chaos.

Korah's rebellion is cited as the definitive expression of contentious self-assertion against legitimate authority; those who follow his pattern abandon themselves to the same destructive dispute-driven inner orientation.
Fervent inner zeal ('hot') is what God desires and finds absent; the church's neither-hot-nor-cold state reveals a will that is uncommitted, exposing the spiritual temperature of the inner person.
Lukewarmness—the absence of any fervent inner heat—provokes God's rejection; the verse shows that an indifferent inner disposition is more repugnant to him than a clear position either way.
God's wrath and the Lamb's wrath are an overwhelming reality that people desperately flee; the verse shows wrath as a divine disposition so terrifying that its objects prefer death to confronting it.
The great day of wrath is imminent and irresistible; the rhetorical question 'who can stand?' conveys that divine wrath carries an overwhelming force against which no creature can hold its ground.
God's wrath arrives as a decisive, timed response to the nations' own raging, functioning as the agent of judgment that separates the rewarded from the destroyed.

The nations' raging is their inner fury expressed collectively; it stands in contrast to God's own wrath that comes as a judicial response, showing human anger as the occasion that triggers divine counter-action.

The dragon's fury is a specific inner reaction to being thwarted by the woman; it drives him outward into active warfare against her offspring, revealing rage as a motivating force that channels into targeted aggression.

God's wrath is portrayed as undiluted and concentrated—poured at full strength—expressing that it is an intense, measured judicial disposition visited on those who worship the beast, resulting in torment.
God's wrath is coupled with fury and described as something Babylon is made to drink to the dregs; it is a remembered, purposeful divine disposition that delivers the full penalty long stored up.

The wrath of God the Almighty is enacted through the conquering Word; it is portrayed as the force behind the winepress of final judgment, making it the supreme expression of divine justice against the nations.
